data.title: South Korean president set to visit China next month, with North Korea high on agenda
data.description:
Lee Jae-myung is set to become the first sitting South Korean president to travel to China since 2019 with a visit scheduled for early January.
Analysts said Lee was expected to engage Beijing on security issues, including North Korea’s nuclear ambitions and the enforcement of international sanctions, while seeking to build on his meeting with Chinese President Xi Jinping in October.
Neither side has confirmed the visit, but preparations are under way, highlighted by a recent strategic vice-ministerial dialogue in Beijing between Park Yoon-joo, South Korea’s first foreign vice-minister, and Ma Zhaoxu, China’s foreign vice-minister.
According to South Korea’s foreign ministry, the two officials discussed critical regional and international concerns and focused on stabilising the situation on the Korean peninsula and addressing maritime disputes in the Yellow Sea, known in South Korea as the West Sea, signalling a mutual effort to revitalise high-level communication channels.


If the trip does go ahead, it will be roughly two months after Xi visited South Korea for the Apec summit, his first trip to the country in 11 years.
Lee is likely to seek Beijing’s help in bringing Pyongyang back to the negotiating table, as communication between the two Koreas remained at a standstill, according to analysts.
Beyond addressing security, the visit is expected to restore stability to bilateral ties, benefiting South Korean businesses and the broader economy.
Niu Xiaoping, a Korean peninsula specialist at the Shanghai Institutes for International Studies, said an exchange of visits by the two countries’ leaders within just three months underscored their commitment to deepening ties.
Prospects are growing for a summit between North Korean leader Kim Jong-un and US President Donald Trump around the time of the American president’s planned April visit to China for talks with Xi.
Trump, who last met Kim in 2019, may seek to re-engage with North Korea to secure a diplomatic victory ahead of the 2026 US midterm congressional elections.
According to Niu, Seoul views Trump’s coming visit to China as a critical moment that could shape the future trajectory of the Korean peninsula.
“Seoul is eager to reach a consensus with Beijing on key issues such as the North Korean nuclear programme and sanctions, while also seeking China’s support and cooperation in facilitating US-North Korea dialogue and inter-Korean talks,” she added.
Kang Jun-young, a professor of Chinese studies at Hankuk University of Foreign Studies in Seoul, said Lee might use his trip to press China to take action on North Korea’s nuclear programme, noting that the issue was notably downplayed in Beijing’s latest policy white paper and given less prominence in Washington’s most recent National Security Strategy.
Kim has ordered an acceleration in the production of missiles and artillery shells, calling for more factories to be built to bolster the North Korean military’s arsenal.
The directive follows Kim’s visit to a shipyard where, according to North Korean state media, an 8,700-tonne nuclear-powered submarine capable of launching surface-to-air missiles is under construction.

Kang said Lee could “deliver a message to China, urging it to place greater focus on the issue of denuclearisation”.
“However, Beijing is in a delicate position,” Kang added. “Pyongyang has declared itself a nuclear weapons state. Any push from China for denuclearisation is likely to fall on deaf ears in Pyongyang. Even if China were to exert pressure, it would likely prove ineffective.”
Meanwhile, as has often been the case, South Korea’s foreign ministry and unification ministry are at odds over their approach to North Korea.
The unification ministry favours a North Korea-focused, inter-Korean bilateral strategy, while the foreign ministry prioritises an international coordination framework led by the US.
The unification ministry is not taking part in the consultative talks between Seoul and Washington that started last week.
According to Niu, South Korea needed to clarify the “pressing issue” of its North Korean policy priorities, “which would also enable better cooperation with China”.
She noted that by visiting China before his scheduled trip to Japan in mid-January, the South Korean leader would appear to be taking a balanced approach and avoiding the perception of favouring Tokyo.
“It is possible that Lee hopes to act as a ‘mediator’ or ‘messenger’, seeking a potential breakthrough for future trilateral cooperation,” Niu said.
Adding to the anticipation, leaders of South Korea’s major conglomerates, including a business delegation of about 200 executives, are expected to accompany Lee on his visit. This would mark the first South Korean business delegation to visit China since 2019.
Liu Ziyang, a professor of global business at Kyonggi University in Suwon, South Korea, said Lee’s visit to China could help solidify and institutionalise the political consensus achieved during his meeting with Xi in October.
“One of Lee’s core objectives for the China trip is to stabilise expectations in bilateral relations, so as to create a more predictable external environment for South Korea’s economy and businesses,” he added.
In the leaders’ last meeting, Xi called for bilateral cooperation in emerging fields such as artificial intelligence, biopharmaceuticals, green industries and the “silver economy”.

Liu noted that cooperation in hi-tech industries could become one of the most practical breakthroughs for China and South Korea, with both countries likely to pursue “selective cooperation under limited competition”.
“South Korea and China face both competition and strong complementarities in sectors such as semiconductors, new materials, artificial intelligence and life sciences,” he said. “Their industrial supply chains have been deeply intertwined for years, making a complete ‘decoupling’ economically and technologically unrealistic.”
Such hi-tech collaboration was driven by industrial efficiency, technological advancement and market demand, he added, making it a natural fit for cooperation while relying less on external conditions.
“Overall, this visit should be understood as a pragmatic, risk management-oriented initiative, helping to provide a more stable and predictable framework for South Korea-China ties,” Liu said.
Despite renewed diplomatic momentum, disputes remain, including Seoul’s US-backed plan for nuclear-powered submarines and Beijing’s expanding presence in the Yellow Sea.
Progress is being made on the nuclear-powered submarine deal, as Seoul plans to equip its submarines with reactors using low-enriched fuel.
South Korea and the US have also agreed to pursue a separate agreement to formalise Seoul’s right to develop nuclear-powered submarines, with working-level discussions scheduled for early next year.
Meanwhile, the North Korean leader has made clear his disapproval of South Korea’s nuclear-powered submarine plan.
According to Pyongyang’s state-run Korean Central News Agency on Thursday, Kim called Seoul’s submarine plan an offensive act and a threat to North Korea, vowing to strengthen his country’s own nuclear submarine strength.
Kang noted that South Korea was a member of the Treaty on the Non-Proliferation of Nuclear Weapons and under strict oversight from international monitoring agencies.
“If Beijing takes a lenient or non-critical approach towards North Korea [regarding its nuclear-powered submarine] while directing criticism at South Korea, it would be unacceptable for Seoul,” he added.
Niu cautioned that if the nuclear-powered submarine and Yellow Sea disputes were not effectively communicated and managed in advance, they could strain bilateral relations.
This article was first published by The Korea Times in partnership with the South China Morning Post.

data.title: ‘First by letting myself move on’: how Hong Kong mum is rebuilding life after Tai Po fire
data.description:
One month after the deadly Tai Po fire, survivors are slowly rebuilding their lives. In the second part of a series on how the community is moving on, Fiona Chow tells the story of one mother’s journey to find stability and hope for her young family amid loss and change. Read the first part here.
Hong Kong mother of two Christy Lee has had to rebuild her life and provide stability for her young children after losing her husband and her home in the Tai Po fire last month.
The social worker, 40, her mother-in-law and two children, aged seven and nine, had crammed into a 150 sq ft flat in transitional housing provided by the government after the inferno at the Wang Fuk Court estate on November 26.
The blaze tore through seven of the Tai Po estate’s eight residential blocks, killing 161 people, injuring 79 others and leaving 5,000 homeless.
Lee has juggled her time over the past month between gathering essential goods from friends, getting aid from organisations and finding a home for the family, which is now solely dependent on her.
Looking around her new 700 sq ft flat in Tai Hang village, a 15-minute drive from her old home at Wang Cheong House – where she had lived with her husband after they got married in 2015 – it sunk in on Friday just how much she had done in the past month.
“I am so tired every day, really. I so wanted to build a stable environment for them as soon as possible,” she said.
She signed a two-year contract with the landlord shortly after the first flat viewing at a discounted rent of HK$8,000 per month.




The flat’s balcony has a mountain view. “It is a place where I can start over, like being reborn. I believe the change of environment will help to change my mood,” Lee said.
She recalled the first few nights in the transitional housing unit at Lok Sin Village in Tai Po, where all four of the family shared a mattress on the floor.
With a million things racing through her head, she was unable to sleep. But her children losing their father hit her the hardest.
“They miss their daddy. I keep thinking to myself, what more can I offer them, how do I give them what they need with minimal changes?” she said, her eyes filling with tears.
“The fire took away what they had before. Something is missing. And what’s most important is what I can do to give them a healthy childhood.”
The children learned of their father’s death through grief counselling, as Lee said she did not have the courage to break the news herself.
When the fire broke out at around 3pm on November 26, Lee’s mother-in-law went to pick up the children from school, while her husband stayed at home.
Her mother-in-law was the last person to speak with her husband over the phone, telling him to “stay awake, don’t sleep” while trapped in the flat.
Firefighters eventually found his body on a staircase and the family identified him through an album of photos of the deceased.
Lee said the children were old enough to be aware of what had happened and asked her questions related to their father’s death, such as: “Why doesn’t God listen to our prayers?”
“I told the kids, as I also comforted myself, that God had indeed listened … their father left without suffering from pain, he just lost his consciousness,” she said.


Lee said she tried to practise positive thinking and avoid drowning in sorrow.
“If I kept on dwelling on the negative thoughts and felt depressed, I wouldn’t have any energy to face the future,” she said.
“And how do I give myself energy? First by letting myself move on.”
She said her current priority was to offer her children a sense of security, and securing a place to stay for the next two years gave them “peace of mind”.
The children loved their new home, she said, adding that she had dedicated one of the three rooms as a playroom for them. Toys gifted by friends were stacked up on the dining table.
Over the weekend, she made multiple trips to different locations to collect second-hand furniture, clothes, essential goods, kitchenware and more from friends and family.
The flat had become cosy and functional within a week.

Lee said there was no way to “recreate” the home they had before, but all she wished was a space where the family could feel at ease and regain a sense of “normality” – meaning the children could go to school, do what they wanted, had space to play in and watch television.
She recalled there was a TV in every bedroom in their old flat, so the couple and the children could lie on their own beds and watch the programmes they enjoyed.
That home was “simple and modest”, as the couple were not materialistic, she said.
“My husband was down-to-earth and we wanted to pass this down to our next generation,” she said.
Lee said she had faith that her husband was watching his family from above and would also feel peaceful that they had settled down.
“At this moment, nothing is more important to me than having a good sleep and keeping the children happy,” she said.

data.title: New benchmarks for Hong Kong language teachers underline policy shift
data.description:
Feel strongly about these letters, or any other aspects of the news? Share your views by emailing us your Letter to the Editor at letters@scmp.com or filling in this Google form. Submissions should not exceed 400 words
As an educator and commentator on language policy in Hong Kong, I read with interest the letter, “Native English speakers shouldn’t be required to take IELTS” (December 12). Some of its claims about IELTS and the Education Bureau’s requirements merit clarification in light of current policy and publicly available data.
On the Education Bureau’s website for the NET Scheme, it is clearly stipulated that applicants should either be native speakers of English or possess native-speaker English competence. The scheme has never been restricted to passport-holding natives; it has always been about demonstrable competence at a very high level.
Following the introduction of the NET grant, the entry requirements have been revised. Under the new grant, NETs are expected to, among other criteria, possess valid IELTS results with an overall band score of 7.5 or above and a speaking band score of 7.5 or above. This sits alongside a broader move to strengthen language standards, as shown by the Education Bureau’s updated Language Proficiency Requirement framework for English and Putonghua teachers.
The explicit recognition of a sufficiently high IELTS (International English Language Testing System) score as satisfying the requirement indicates a broader policy shift towards common, externally benchmarked standards across the system, rather than an isolated rule aimed only at NETs.
Your correspondent suggests that IELTS is essentially for non-native speakers. In fact, guidance material from IELTS partners makes clear that native English speakers are also required to sit the test in various immigration and professional contexts, including for certain visas to countries such as Canada, the UK, Australia and New Zealand, where authorities insist on standardised test evidence rather than assumptions based on nationality or schooling.
There is certainly room for debate over whether demanding fresh IELTS results from experienced native speaker teachers is proportionate or necessary, especially when the NET Scheme already defines native speaker competence in detail and many NETs have long, successful teaching records. Yet the existence of an IELTS requirement for NETs is not, in itself, anomalous. It mirrors global norms in which even native speakers are expected to demonstrate their skills through standardised tests for high-stakes professional roles.
In the context of Hong Kong’s ambition to position itself as an international education hub and to promote the “Study in Hong Kong” brand, robust language requirements for teachers have become even more important, as they are intended to maintain high English standards across the sector. The real policy question is how to balance that aim with proportionality and practicality, rather than to dismiss the requirement as unnecessary or “complete nonsense”.
Ilnur Minakhmetov, Yau Ma Tei
Residents’ well-being must matter in building renovation
I have lived in Hong Kong for over 47 years, and during this time very little seems to have changed when it comes to building renovation practices and oversight.
I have always been concerned that when compounds are being renovated, all the buildings involved are covered by scaffolding and shrouded in netting for lengthy periods of time, even though usually only one building is being worked on at any one time. This not only poses a fire threat, but is also a massive inconvenience and security risk for residents, not to mention the degradation of their living conditions.
This method of working appears to prioritise the convenience of construction companies over the safety and well-being of the people who live in the estates. The terrible tragedy in Tai Po makes it clear that this must be addressed and changed for the better.
In addition, how much supervision is there of internal work carried out by owners of apartments? In some cases, walls are removed and extensive structural work is done, which disturb other residents for months at a stretch and may threaten the integrity of the building.
Hong Kong’s building renovation policies need to be reviewed to better protect its residents.
Dorothy Wong, North Point
Making Christmas meaningful again
The frenetic gift-buying in the lead-up to Christmas yet again testifies to its annual commercialisation that guts Christ’s birth of its true worth.
Christmas online shopping mania to the tune of billions, anxious hand-wringing about delayed deliveries that don’t make it in time for the Christmas tree gift unwrapping, the mad rush to avoid shopping mall crowds to buy gifts online, all testify to Australia’s erring consumerist belief that physical gifts are the truest manifestation of goodwill towards our dearest and fairest.
How about emailing a snapshot of a handwritten letter sharing our heartfelt thoughts or an e-card that supplants hand- or plane-delivery to neighbours or recipients on the other side of the world?
This year my family has banned Christmas gifting because we believe that this is not the true meaning of Christ’s birth. It’s a small first step away from colossal gifting, kids excepted of course. The saved funds will be disbursed to those without, in need, in grief, in hunger and in pain.
Joseph Ting, Brisbane, Australia

data.title: Singapore in 2025: election fever, courtroom drama, pickleball mania
data.description:
Singapore saw plenty of drama unfold this year, from pickleball courts to the Supreme Court.
The city state was gripped by election fever in May, when the People’s Action Party maintained its dominance at the polls.
On the trade front, Washington’s imposition of a 10 per cent tariff on Singapore elicited a strong reaction from Prime Minister Lawrence Wong. The jury is still out on whether US President Donald Trump would introduce levies on pharmaceuticals and semiconductors, which could deliver a significant blow to Singapore’s economy next year if implemented.
Here are the most widely read Singapore stories This Week in Asia covered in 2025:
PAP dominance
The ruling PAP continued its dominance, which predates Singapore’s independence, winning 87 out of the 97 seats on offer and securing 65.57 per cent of the vote, thwarting observers’ predictions that the opposition could make inroads amid a growing appetite for diversity in parliament.
The party’s performance also bucked the trend of a new prime minister seeing a drop in vote share during his first election, with Wong securing a more than 4 percentage point vote swing in his party’s favour in his first general election as prime minister.
Wong campaigned on having the best team to steer the country through volatile times and economic headwinds.
The PAP’s closest ballot battles unfolded in the Tampines and Jalan Kayu constituencies against the Workers’ Party (WP), which observers said solidified WP’s status as the main opposition party. The WP retained its 10 elected seats and repeated its 2020 feat of winning more votes than the PAP in the eight constituencies it contested.
During the election campaign, Wong held a press conference where he called on all political parties to renounce mixing religion with politics and foreign interference in Singapore’s affairs, referring to a Malaysia-based Singaporean Islamic teacher who claimed to have spoken to WP’s Malay candidates.

In a pickle
Out of the political arena and into the pickleball court, the booming popularity of the sport – similar to tennis but played on a badminton-sized court with plastic balls – became a bane for some residents who complained of noise disturbances.
There are more than 5,000 active players in Singapore, and bookings at 30 public courts have more than tripled since 2023, as authorities and operators grapple with balancing between the game’s community appeal and the challenges of a densely packed city.
To deal with pickleball-related noise complaints at public housing estates, some town councils have banned the sport or restricted play times, while others have taken more drastic measures, such as welding metal badminton nets, which are higher than pickleball nets.
Meanwhile, public and private entities are ramping up the supply of courts.

Unfriendly tariffs
When Singapore was handed a baseline 10 per cent levy for its exports to the US as part of Trump’s so-called Liberation Day tariffs, it prompted strong words by Wong, who called Washington’s move “not actions one does to a friend”.
During a ministerial statement in parliament in April, Wong argued that if the levies were reciprocal and aimed at trade surplus nations, the tariff for Singapore should be zero.
The US enjoyed a surplus of US$2.8 billion in its goods trade with Singapore in 2024, an 84.8 per cent rise from the previous year, according to data from the Office of the United States Trade Representative. The countries also has a free-trade agreement in place for more than two decades.
In response to the tariff move and other developments, Wong convened a task force on economic resilience, chaired by Deputy Prime Minister Gan Kim Yong, who was dubbed the “Taskforce Man”. Singapore’s key semiconductor and pharmaceutical exports remain vulnerable to Washington’s sectoral tariffs, which have yet to be announced.

Courtroom drama
The year also saw the conclusion of two closely watched criminal cases; one involving property tycoon Ong Beng Seng, who was embroiled in a corruption probe against former transport minister S Iswaran and the other surrounding opposition leader Pritam Singh from WP.
Ong, who is credited with bringing Formula One night racing to Singapore in 2008, was fined S$30,000 (US$23,285) in August after being convicted of abetting Iswaran in obstructing justice. The 79-year-old Malaysian billionaire avoided prison because of poor health.
Iswaran was sentenced to a year’s imprisonment in October last year and finished serving his time in June.

Meanwhile, Singh claimed trial and appealed against his conviction related to the accusation that he lied to a parliamentary committee formed to look into falsehoods told by former WP MP Raeesah Khan in 2021. Singapore’s highest court, however, upheld the S$14,000 (US$10,800) fine imposed on him earlier this month.
Singh may not be out of the woods yet, with Leader of the House Indranee Rajah announcing on December 17 that parliament would discuss an appropriate response to Singh’s conviction, calling the court’s findings “disturbing”.
In a statement posted on social media on the same day, the WP confirmed it has started the party’s “internal processes” following the conclusion of the court case.
Oxley saga resurfaces
The former home of founding prime minister Lee Kuan Yew was gazetted by the government earlier this month, a pivotal moment that seemingly signalled the conclusion of a years-long family dispute between his children.
The bungalow at 38 Oxley Road had been the centre of clashes between former prime minister Lee Hsien Loong, who favoured preserving the property, and his younger siblings Lee Hsien Yang and the late Lee Wei Ling, who called for its demolition, citing their father’s wishes.

The National Heritage Board maintained that the site was worthy of preservation for its historic significance. The bungalow was the site of crucial meetings attended by the PAP’s founding members in the 1950s, when Singapore was still a British colony, and in the years before independence in 1965.
The government said all traces of the Lee family’s private living spaces from the interior of the house would be removed, out of respect for the late Lee Kuan Yew’s wishes to protect their privacy.
Lee Hsien Yang and Lee Wei Ling first made public the conflict between the three siblings in 2017, accusing their elder brother, who was then prime minister, of trying to block the demolition of the house for political gain.
Deadly neighbours
In September, the city state was gripped by a murder case in which a 66-year-old man was arrested after a noise dispute turned deadly at a public housing block in northern Singapore.
Koh Ah Hwee allegedly attacked Nguyen Phuong Tra, 30, and her husband with a knife. Nguyen died of injuries in hospital. Neighbours reportedly said both households had been feuding over noise for years.
The incident fuelled a debate on whether Singapore’s framework for resident disputes was sufficient, after it was revealed Nguyen had applied for community mediation, but Koh did not accept the invitation.

More than 80 per cent of the country’s population lives in high-rise public flats, and there were over 2,500 noise-related reports on average every month in the first half of the year.
A new government unit set up to deal with neighbour disputes was launched earlier this year and is currently undergoing a one-year pilot in Tampines. The unit has the authority to deploy noise sensors, issue directives requiring attendance at mediation, and hand out abatement orders.
Child abuse case and a ministerial apology
Lapses in Singapore’s child protection system prompted a rare ministerial apology after it was revealed that authorities and social service agencies could have done more to prevent a child’s horrific death from abuse.
Four-year-old Megan Khung died in February 2020 after suffering a year of abuse at the hands of her mother, Foo Li Ping, and the woman’s then-boyfriend, Wong Shi Xiang. The child had been forced to sleep in a planter box and was hit with a hosepipe. She died after being punched in the stomach by Wong.
The government set up an independent review panel to look into the case, and findings released in October showed that social workers mischaracterised Megan’s injuries and police officers and child protection specialists failed to follow up to check on her.

In a rare move, Minister for Social and Family Development Masagos Zulkifli apologised and said the agencies could have done more.
Two police officers were disciplined internally after it was found that they did not follow established processes. One has since resigned, while the other was subjected to formal disciplinary actions, found guilty and fined.
Foo was sentenced to 19 years’ jail, and Wong was jailed for 30 years and given 17 strokes of the cane.
Train delays and disruptions
Acting transport minister Jeffrey Siow told parliament in September that “zero disruptions” across Singapore’s rail network was an unrealistic expectation, amid mounting criticism over delays and disruptions on the city state’s subway lines.
Siow said the government was boosting efforts to strengthen rail reliability.
There were at least 15 delays and interruptions across the MRT and LRT networks between July and September, according to local media reports.

Based on data from the Land Transport Authority (LTA), the network’s mean kilometres between failure – a key measure of train reliability – fell to 1.6 million train-km in the 12 months to June, the lowest since 2020.
Earlier this month, commuters were left confused at train stations after they realised that their rail journey could be extended by up to 20 minutes because of a train fault. Typically, rail operators would announce delays on their social media platforms, but there were no alerts on that occasion.
LTA said the next day that operators would prioritise alerts on delays by relaying them to passengers who were directly affected at stations.

data.title: 1 killed in Japan as 50-vehicle pile-up leads to crash on icy expressway
data.description:
A massive crash in snowy weather killed one person and injured 26 on an expressway in Japan late on Friday as the country kicked off its end-of-the-year holiday season.
The Gunma prefectural highway police said on Saturday that the pile-up on the Kan-etsu Expressway started with a collision between two trucks in the town of Minakami, about 160km (100 miles) northwest of Tokyo.
A 77-year-old woman from Tokyo died, police said. Out of the 26 injured, five were reported to be in serious condition.
The crash of the trucks blocked parts of the expressway, and cars coming from behind them were unable to brake on the snowy surface. More than 50 vehicles were involved in the pile-up, police said.
A fire erupted at the far end of the pile-up, spreading to more than a dozen vehicles, some of which were completely burned. Nobody was injured from the fire, which was extinguished about seven hours later, police said.
A warning about heavy snow was in effect late on Friday, when many Japanese started their year-end and new year holidays.
A section of the expressway was subsequently closed, with a row of badly damaged vehicles, some charred beyond recognition, clogging the outbound lane.
A driver in his 60s said his truck hit the median after swerving to avoid a car ahead of him. He also heard the sound of explosions four times behind him.
“The ice made it difficult for me to control the steering wheel,” he said. “I feared for my life.”
Additional reporting by Kyodo

data.title: Hong Kong’s Operation Santa Claus strengthened by Morgan Stanley’s enduring support
data.description:
Morgan Stanley’s fundraising drive for Operation Santa Claus (OSC) has kept the firm on course to remain the campaign’s top corporate supporter for the ninth consecutive year, extending a partnership of nearly three decades that has engaged employees across divisions, seniority levels and borders.
According to Gokul Laroia, CEO of Asia at Morgan Stanley, the global financial services firm’s enduring support for OSC reflected its long-term commitment to giving back to the community, as illustrated by this year’s “building bridges” campaign theme.
“For us, building bridges is about creating pathways for empathy, understanding and shared responsibility,” Laroia said.
“Through Operation Santa Claus and our internal initiatives such as ‘Season for Good’, we are strengthening connections that empower individuals, uplift communities and inspire lasting change.”
Morgan Stanley has supported OSC for 29 years, raising more than HK$80 million (US$10.3 million) over that time. This year alone, the bank contributed HK$8.8 million, benefiting Kids4Kids, the Love 21 Foundation, Mind HK, the other 13 OSC beneficiaries and relief efforts linked to the deadly fire in Tai Po.
Laroia noted that OSC was known for its trust and transparency, adding that employees felt their contributions were managed responsibly and that this confidence strongly influenced their willingness to give.
That trust also shapes how staff take part. Rather than relying solely on major donations, the bank encourages participation through structured internal campaigns, most visibly under its global ‘Season for Good’ initiative.
In Hong Kong, this includes long-running events such as the managing directors’ charity auction and the charity bake auction.
“These activities have become part of how we work together. They bring colleagues from different teams together around a shared purpose and a sense of responsibility for the community we operate in,” Laroia said.
Beyond fundraising, Morgan Stanley employees also give their time and effort through volunteer work with partner organisations.
Laroia said staff had helped pack food items at food banks and assembled furniture for families moving into transitional housing in the wake of the last month’s fire, which killed 161 people and displaced around 5,000 residents.

“Our colleagues rolled up their sleeves to help families directly. That involvement keeps the focus on people and helps everyone understand why the work matters,” he said.
He added that OSC’s mission to unlock the spirit of giving aligned closely with the firm’s broader focus on youth well-being and education.
“Operation Santa Claus connects corporations, non-profit organisations and communities in a way that allows resources to reach where they are most needed,” Laroia said.
While the annual fundraising drive follows a familiar structure, this year’s campaign was shaped by events outside the usual calendar.
After the devastating fire, Morgan Stanley worked with OSC to launch an emergency appeal within days.
“That trust enabled us to act quickly. With OSC’s support, we launched an emergency appeal and matched employee donations dollar for dollar. That response will help provide families with essential resources as they work to rebuild their homes and lives,” Laroia said.

He said colleagues across Hong Kong and mainland China responded swiftly and the response reaffirmed the value of the partnership.
“Giving back strengthens communities,” he said. “When people act together, the impact carries forward.”
Jointly established by the Post and public broadcaster RTHK in 1988, OSC has raised around HK$396 million for more than 365 charity projects, benefiting more than 100,000 people throughout the years.
In addition to its annual fundraising drive, the campaign is leading an emergency appeal to meet the specific needs of residents affected by the Tai Po inferno.
All proceeds will support this year’s 13 OSC-funded charity projects and community partners involved in the fire relief efforts.
Click here to learn more about how to donate to OSC.

data.title: Shorter quarantine time drives rise in pet imports from mainland China to Hong Kong
data.description:
Hong Kong has seen an increase in applications to import pets after the city cut the quarantine period for cats and dogs from mainland China from 120 to 30 days.
The Agriculture, Fisheries and Conservation Department (AFCD) also said that a greater number of quarantine units at its new Kai Tak complex had further reduced waiting times for pet owners relocating to Hong Kong and hoping to bring their animals with them.
The department said it received 291 applications for pet quarantine in the first 10 months of this year, covering 196 cats and 95 dogs. This compares with 283 applications in 2024.
Veterinary officer Bonnie Yu Hui-wai said that of the nearly 300 applications, 260 involved pets imported from the mainland.
“We have already issued 101 certificates to pets imported from the mainland according to the requirements under Group IIIA,” she said this week, referring to a category in the city’s animal import control system that classifies places based on their rabies risk.
Last December, the department announced a new rabies-risk classification for pets entering the city from different countries and regions, placing Macau and Lithuania in a new Group IIIA.
The mainland, previously in the highest-risk Group IIIB, was moved to IIIA in June, cutting the mandatory quarantine from 120 days to 30 days. Pets must be microchipped, vaccinated against rabies and other diseases, and pass a blood test.
The new animal management and welfare complex at Kai Tak began operating last November and has 20 quarantine rooms each for dogs and cats, compared with 10 and 14 units respectively at the old To Kwa Wan facility.
Between October 2024 and September this year, 57 dogs and 55 cats were housed at the department’s quarantine centre.
Yu said the additional facilities and shorter quarantine period meant shorter waiting times and lower costs for pet owners, who could be reunited with their animals sooner.
“Our waiting time has shortened because of the increased number of quarantine units, and with the new classification of Group IIIA, the quarantine time can be shortened if pets meet the requirements,” she said.
“Most pets from Group III in the past were from the mainland, so with this new classification, the turnover rate for the quarantine units has increased while it’s also cheaper for the owners.”
The current daily cost of quarantine at the facility is HK$90 (US$11.57) for dogs and HK$42 for cats.
The waiting time for a quarantine place is now around three to six months for dogs and 12 months for cats.
This compares with October 2024, when the average waiting time was 12 to 15 months for dogs and 15 to 18 months for cats.
The new building, complete with pet-safe calming diffusers and soothing classical music, also features enlarged holding facilities for 130 dogs and 303 cats, including surrendered animals, strays awaiting adoption, and animals rescued from abuse or smuggling cases.
In the first 10 months of this year, the department recorded nine cases of pets being brought into the city without permits and seized 14 animals. It seized 11 animals in the whole of last year.

Checks by the Post found businesses promoting quarantine-free, same-day pet transfers between the mainland and Hong Kong on the social media platform RedNote.
According to the advertised services, pets are placed in crates on the back seats of private cars, with operators promising fresh air and point-to-point transport.
“We offer safe and legitimate transport by land without quarantine and same-day arrival,” a seller told the Post.
The business quoted the Post 1,400 yuan (US$199) for transporting a pet between Shenzhen and Hong Kong and asked only for details such as a photo and the pet’s gender for registration.
When asked about the legality of the service, the seller claimed that the pet would be sent through “proper channels”, and that it “would not get caught”.
However, when queried about moving a medium-sized dog, the seller said it would only handle cats and small dogs under 13kg (29lbs).
“If it’s too big, it is difficult to transport across nowadays,” the seller said.
Yu said the department would work with the Customs and Excise Department and police to share intelligence and carry out joint operations at the airport and border crossings.
“All pets entering Hong Kong should undergo quarantine for the benefit of the animal’s health and public health,” she said, noting that the mainland was still not completely free of rabies.
“We are aware some owners send their pets to the mainland on a day trip to receive [medical or aesthetic] treatments and they pose a huge risk if they are in contact with other animals.”
Yu warned owners against using such services, saying the quarantine period helped prevent animal diseases, including rabies, from being brought into Hong Kong, which had been rabies-free for decades.
“If they do not go through the proper channels, they might not be vaccinated against rabies and if infected, it poses almost a 100 per cent death risk in both humans and animals,” she said.

data.title: From 12-hour races to humpback whales: 12-man Hong Kong canoe team make Pacific history
data.description:
A group of 12 paddlers from Hong Kong have become the first team from the city to complete the Hawaiki Nui Va’a – an annual three-day outrigger canoe race in French Polynesia and one of the world’s toughest ocean endurance events.
The team, known as the Aloha Adventure Squad, spent eight months training before travelling to Tahiti in October for the event, which covers about 128 kilometres (80 miles) across four islands: Huahine, Raiatea, Taha’a and Bora Bora.
Most of the squad met through the city’s local outrigger community.
Seb Doolan, the coach and also one of the team members, took charge of the group three years ago after a disappointing domestic season and began preparing them for international competition.
“The team’s name ‘Aloha’ started as just a greeting from Hawaii,” Doolan, a 33-year-old barber, said. “But it came to symbolise something deeper: a self-funded team that trains before sunrise, without sponsorship.”

Their path to Tahiti ran through Hawaii’s Molokai Hoe, a 66-kilometre channel crossing between Molokai and Oahu, and often called the world championship of outrigger canoeing.
Part of the team finished that race with other members from Hong Kong in six hours, 10 minutes and 11 seconds, finishing 54th of 106 teams – and the fastest ever by a Hong Kong crew.
“That result showed we belonged on that stage,” Doolan said. “It gave us the confidence to take on Tahiti.”
Ranging in age from their early 30s to 60, the paddlers come from a mix of professions, including finance, law, barbering and sports coaching.

They trained up to five days a week, often starting at 5am in Deep Water Bay, and spending two to three hours paddling around Lamma, Cheung Chau or Hong Kong Island before heading to work.
“Even under Typhoon Signal No 3, we still went out,” crew member Hugh Allman said. “That is how you prepare for real ocean conditions.”
During the race, there were no substitutions – all 12 paddlers remained in the canoe throughout, carrying their own water and nutrition supplies. The team finished in 12 hours, 11 minutes and six seconds, completing the race after battling open-sea swells and mental fatigue.
“The toughest part was mental,” Doolan said. “You’re paddling for hours, staring at an island that does not seem to get any closer. I can see it is not that far away, but actually, you are looking at the same thing for a long time.”
Their persistence brought moments of beauty too.

“On the final leg to Bora Bora, a humpback whale and its calf surfaced just five metres away from us,” Allman, who works in sales, recalled. “It was one of those moments you’ll never forget.
“Hong Kong has its own charm, surrounded by the ocean, 20 minutes from skyscrapers to the open sea,” he said. “We proved this city can be part of the world’s biggest ocean races. That is something to be proud of.”
The team comprised canoeists of several nationalities: English, American, Chilean, Spanish, Romanian, Italian and Hongkongers.
“I am very proud of that; that is cool,” Allman said. “Maybe we are not all Hong Kong by blood, but we all consider this place our home. Most of us have been home for more than six years, all very proud to live in Hong Kong and represent Hong Kong.”
The team hope their experience can inspire more people to take up canoeing.

“When you are passionate about something, you naturally want to share it,” Allman said. “That is what happened to me. I saw these guys train so hard before, so I did too and it made me proud.
“The whole goal is to let your passion inspire others. When you actually see that happen – when your energy motivates people to take on big challenges – that is when it all feels worth it.”
After the challenging race, some of the team members are already considering what comes next – perhaps a paddle from Hong Kong to the Philippines, or even around Taiwan island.
For them, the adventure continues.

data.title: From hope to hard numbers: why China stocks will need profits to boost the bull run
data.description:
After a year-long rally in Chinese stocks powered by multiple expansion as confidence recovered, investors now say that the bull run could find fresh legs in 2026 – this time underpinned by stronger earnings growth.
Supportive macro policy, the push for technological self-reliance and Beijing’s effort to retire obsolete capacity in parts of the green industry are expected to lift margins and profits.
That would make earnings, not valuation, the key driver for equities in the year ahead.
With much of the confidence-driven re-rating already banked, extending the rally will depend on fundamentals: steadier nominal growth, easing producer-price deflation and stronger pricing discipline in oversupplied sectors.
UBS Group predicted that the profit growth rate would accelerate to 8 per cent from 6 per cent in 2025, because of faster nominal economic expansion and a smaller decline in producer prices, while JPMorgan Chase expected growth to range between 9 and 15 per cent.
A switch to earnings growth from multiple expansion would put Chinese equities on a firmer footing, particularly after positives from the nation’s dominant position in the tech industry, waning geopolitical tensions and monetary easing in the US have been largely priced in.
While both benchmarks of Chinese and Hong Kong stocks have made their way into the ranks of the world’s best-performing markets this year, the outperformance means that stock gains may stall with little room for valuation expansion to run, unless earnings growth catches up.


“Chinese equities look set to enjoy solid tailwinds in 2026,” said Gary Dugan, CEO of The Global CIO Office that advises family offices and ultra-high-net-worth individuals.
“The backdrop to China’s equity market is encouraging and has longevity,” he said. “The combination of supportive policy, improving profitability in key strategic sectors and still-depressed multiples offers a more promising set-up than many of China’s critics care to admit.”
An annual economic work conference that wrapped up this month struck a dovish note on policy setting for next year. The continuity of fiscal expansion and monetary loosening has stirred speculation that the government will spend more on investments and the central bank will deliver further cuts in interest rates.
At the conference, President Xi Jinping and other top leaders also prioritised advancing tech innovation and reviving domestic demand on the policy agenda, suggesting that more resources will be tilting in favour of those emerging industries.
US money manager Invesco said that the policy approach would help China’s economy to expand by about 5 per cent next year, aligning with the annual growth target over the past five years and getting the latest five-year plan, which aims to double per capita gross domestic product by 2030, off to a solid start.
UBS expected a further increase in investor risk appetite, saying that growth stocks would outperform value ones and that cyclical companies would beat the defensive sector.
Forecasts for the performances of key indexes were also heartening.
JPMorgan Chase said that Chinese stocks might deliver double-digit gains in 2026, buoyed by accelerating earnings growth as Beijing’s so-called anti-involution drive boosted profitability by weeding out unnecessary capacity across sectors from electric vehicles and photovoltaics to the internet.
The MSCI China Index, a gauge widely used by global investors to track Chinese stocks, could rise 18 per cent through the end of 2026, while the CSI 300 Index of yuan-traded onshore stocks on the mainland’s exchanges would probably gain 12 per cent, it said.
CCB International, the investment banking unit of China Construction Bank, anticipated the Hang Seng Index to climb to as high as 30,500, implying an 18 per cent gain from the measure’s last close, aided by a stronger yuan and Beijing’s policy front-loading in the first year heading into China’s five-year plan.
“China and other Asian markets have already delivered strong performances in 2025, and multiple asset classes are poised to carry this momentum into 2026,” said David Chao, a strategist at Invesco with US$2.1 trillion of assets under management.
“Against a backdrop of favourable policy support and improving fundamentals, Asian markets offer attractive opportunities for investors looking to diversify and rebalance portfolios in the coming year.”
Not everyone, however, is that sanguine about the outlook.
Morgan Stanley was restrained in its forecast for the MSCI China gauge, which it said would finish 2026 at 90, a level that would put the gain within a single-digit percentage. Its targets for the Hang Seng Index and the CSI 300 were also subdued compared with the outlook from its peers.
“As a result of the re-rating, we believe the current market valuation has largely reflected the perception shift of global investors from China as a deflationary and macro-slowdown story towards one of innovation and hard-core technology breakthroughs,” said Laura Wang, a strategist at the US investment bank.
Wang said that she would turn more positive on stocks if the China-US relationship improved further, Beijing made a more decisive fiscal pivot by tackling the housing inventory and more breakthroughs were made in the technology industry.
The outperformance of Chinese stocks in 2025 has been beyond the expectations of most investors, who had remained cautious at the start of the year on jitters about the scale of policy support.
The abrupt rise of DeepSeek, whose low-cost chatbot could match ChatGPT in performance, the resilience of exports and a cooling of tariff tensions with the US have reset expectations and added to conviction that the worst for equities is already behind them.
The Hang Seng Index has jumped almost 30 per cent this year, and the gain on the mainland’s benchmark CSI 300 Index is close to 20 per cent, with a gauge of a Nasdaq-like technology board on the Shanghai exchange posting an eye-popping 37 per cent.


In comparison, the S&P 500 has risen 18 per cent over the same span.
The rally has been characterised by broadening participation, with foreign investors returning to the Chinese markets in pursuit of an alternative investment as US stocks trade at the top end of their historical valuation ranges.
Evidence of their rising appetite could be seen in the global fund managers that turned into active buyers of Hong Kong’s initial public offerings (IPO), reviving demand on the primary market of new flotations and subsequently lifting sentiment on the secondary market when those listings began trading.
BlackRock and Temasek Holdings of Singapore were among the cornerstone investors of Hong Kong’s biggest stock sales this year, including the US$5.3 billion offering of lithium battery maker Contemporary Amperex Technology and the US$1.98 billion one of heavy machinery maker Sany Heavy Industry.
“Global investors are coming back. You can see their enthusiasm in both primary and secondary markets,” said Bin Shi, head of China equities at UBS Asset Management.
“Despite the rally, we are far from overheated, with both valuations and investor participation still below historical peaks. If the economy cooperates, there’s more upside ahead. The next stage for Chinese equities will be driven by fundamentals.”
Shi said that he would focus on pharmaceutical and technology stocks, which were likely to retain their leadership on the market in 2026 as industry sentiment remained resilient amid continuing fundamental improvements.
Against the backdrop of monetary easing in the US and the rising concentration risk from American tech companies, Chinese stocks are a good option for diversification. Despite the Washington-imposed tech restrictions of recent years, China remains a leader in fields ranging from artificial intelligence and smart manufacturing to automation and humanoid robotics.
The world’s second-largest economy also has a dominant position in global supply chains, controlling more than 60 per cent of the world’s solar capacity and manufacturing nearly 80 per cent of the batteries that power electric vehicles, according to Dugan at The Global CIO Office.
The onshore exchanges in Shanghai and Shenzhen, which used to take a back seat to Hong Kong and the US for listings of emerging industries, now host a slew of profitable tech companies that would benefit from the national strategy that calls for industrial self-reliance, he said.
Even after the gains, Chinese tech companies’ valuation edge over their US counterparts is conspicuous.
The Hang Tech Index, which comprises China’s biggest players in the industry including Alibaba Group Holding (owner of the Post), Tencent Holdings and Baidu, trades at 25.9 times estimated earnings, while the multiple for the Nasdaq 100 index is 31.3 and that for the Magnificent Seven US tech companies is 37.5, according to Bloomberg data.




“We expect Hong Kong and China stocks to continue to gain in 2026 given relatively comfortable valuations,” said Lorraine Tan, director of equity research at Morningstar. “AI is expected to remain a key driver for tech stocks, with continued investment in hardware and software. Major internet companies like Alibaba and Tencent are likely to have an advantage in AI capabilities.”
Chinese tech stocks were less vulnerable to pullbacks than their US counterparts, due to their more balanced weightings of the broader market, according to CCB International.
The Hang Seng Tech Index represented about a fifth of the value of the entire Hong Kong market, while the ratio of the Nasdaq 100 and the whole of US equities was 41 per cent, it said.
US investors perceive the extreme concentration as a key risk that would prevent the gains from broadening to other parts of the market.
For Shenwan Hongyuan Group, a leading Chinese brokerage, the rally in Chinese stock has yet to run its course, as a low-interest-rate environment is set to fuel further rotation of the nation’s whopping 163 trillion yuan (US$23.2 trillion) of household savings into risk assets in 2026.
Its proprietary index tracking household asset allocations showed that the migration to stocks had bottomed out in the July-to-September period, suggesting more scope for an uptick after such capital flows drove the Shanghai Composite Index to a decade high this year.
The Federal Reserve cut the benchmark borrowing cost by 25 basis points for a third straight time this month, a move that may prompt a similar reduction by the People’s Bank of China to further lower yields across fixed-income products.
Victoria Mio at Janus Henderson saw a multi-year investment opportunity in Chinese stocks, as policymakers mapped out a road map in the high-stakes five-year plan for the nation to pivot to high-quality and innovation-driven growth.
“China is far from being uninvestable,” said Mio, head of Greater China equities at the asset-management firm that oversees US$484 billion of assets under management.
“Investing in China today requires a new playbook. Disciplined sector and stock selection, a deep understanding of policy priorities, and patience to capture structural opportunities are prerequisites for a long-term rewarding investment in China equities.”

data.title: Traditional Chinese parenting 101: sending a child on a solo mission
data.description:
“Be brave,” I told my 12-year-old daughter at the station steps. The words were as much for me as for her.
Train pass – check. Phone, wallet, debit card – check. Rendezvous after her piano lesson – check. “And don’t miss your stop!” It was our third run-through of the checklist.
“Dad, you’re such a fusspot. I’ll be fine,” she said, cringing as she hoisted her schoolbag. We’d made that trip to her piano teacher’s studio, near Town Hall station in the Sydney central business district, many times before. She swiped her pass and strode through the turnstiles. For a moment, as she began her maiden solo journey – just before she vanished into the crowd – she seemed taller, shoulders back, as if the station platform had given her a little lift. And I recognised in her air the same exhilaration I’d once felt somewhere in the streets of Kowloon 35 years earlier.
I was eight when I visited Hong Kong with my family in 1990. My grandfather, a businessman then in the furniture trade, had gathered us for Christmas at the Mariners’ Club in Tsim Sha Tsui, a tall blue building that once stood on the southern tip of the Kowloon peninsula. On our second morning, he summoned me to his room. I remember the purposeful glint in his eye – the look of a veteran tutor issuing an important assignment.
“When I was your age, I fetched my grandpa’s newspaper every morning. On my own.” He tapped a finger on the coffee table. “I’ll show you the route today, walk it with you tomorrow; after that, you’ll do it yourself.”
It was his version of “watch once, try once with supervision, then go solo” – a teaching method common to his generation, forged by experience, hard knocks and the belief that children should be toughened up for the world. Years later, I would encounter this tough-love approach again in medical school.

We set out before breakfast. Our hotel sat beside Signal Hill Garden, its stairs rising to a hill that overlooked the neighbourhood. We’d visited the lookout the day we arrived. The newsagent was inside a mall across a T-intersection, about 200 metres away. Because I wasn’t allowed to jaywalk, we went to the next crossing and doubled back. The route seemed simple enough. The following morning, with grandpa by my side, I navigated it correctly.
On the third morning, I went alone. I found the newsagent without trouble, but on the way back I took a wrong turn. The streets turned unfamiliar. I had no phone, no map and I couldn’t speak Cantonese. I gripped the newspaper tighter. Beyond a canyon of towers, I caught the outline of Signal Hill. Remembering how it overlooked the district, I entered the park and climbed to the top. From there, I saw the hotel’s familiar blue and found my way back.
A little late, breathless but triumphant, I stepped into the lobby clutching the newspaper. My grandfather was waiting. When I told him I’d lost my way and used Signal Hill to get my bearings, his concerned expression softened into a small, satisfied smile. My mother, who hadn’t been privy to the mission, was not at all impressed.

That contrast was formative; it still guides my parenting. My grandfather, very much in the traditional Chinese mould, believed independence came from shouldering responsibility early; to him, controlled risk was training for adulthood. My mother, a new migrant from Shanghai raising her only child in late-20th-century Australia, absorbed the safety-first ethos of an era defined by “stranger danger”, checklists and caution. Back home, I was mostly wrapped in cotton wool; that day in Kowloon was my coming of age.
Modern Chinese parenting balances two instincts. Confucian values prize duty to family, respect for elders and teachers, and the cultivation of character. Parents today, especially in the diaspora, wrestle with equally important Western ideals of choice, individuality and freedom.
Growing up in Australia, I lived in the in-between: neither fully bound by one tradition nor free of the other. Grandpa never said so, but I sensed he thought small struggles – like getting a little lost – were the best way to build character. Mum, by contrast, insisted that I check in with her whenever I went out. This tension endures in how I parent. I want my children to stand tall, yet know they are not alone; to take risks, but with safety nets.

Encouraging responsibility is vital for growth. My daughter’s solo train ride wasn’t in Kowloon, but letting go was harder than I’d imagined. Like my mother, I reached for checklists; like my grandfather, I knew it was time to let her find her own way. After her first solo mission, my daughter’s face shone with the same pride I’d felt on Signal Hill. Soon her younger brother will want his turn. I won’t send him into Kowloon. I’ll look for his version instead: small, safe adventures that help him find his bearings, like going to school camp or mapping the route on our next family holiday.
Parents forever walk the line between safety and freedom. Every generation shifts that line. For my wife, our children and me, one truth endures: independence grows from trust. The art of parenting – Chinese or Western – is to teach, to trust and to let go.
